questions.question: Why is an explicit order state machine better than one generic status field?
options: Because recovery behavior and allowed transitions become visible instead of implied.
Because databases cannot store short status strings efficiently.
Because state machines eliminate all retries automatically.
Because one status field prevents partial failures.
questions.explanation: Complex workflows need explicit semantics for progress and recovery.

questions.question: What is the main reason to keep an event history for orders?
options: It supports debugging, replay, and operator understanding of what happened.
It lets you remove idempotency keys from downstream calls.
It guarantees every order can be rolled back synchronously.
It replaces the need for terminal states.
questions.explanation: Order history is the evidence trail for distributed workflow behavior.

questions.question: What is the most credible response when payment succeeds but inventory reservation fails?
options: Enter an explicit compensation path such as refund or release rather than hiding the mismatch.
Mark the order complete and fix it later manually.
Delete the order row and retry checkout from scratch automatically.
Assume the inventory system will eventually reconcile itself.
questions.explanation: Partial success needs modeled compensation, not wishful rollback.

questions.question: Why should transition handling be idempotent?
options: Because callbacks and retries can arrive multiple times or out of order.
Because idempotency reduces the number of workflow states required to two.
Because only successful transitions can repeat.
Because workflow engines never persist duplicate events.
questions.explanation: Repeated external input is a normal condition in distributed workflows.

questions.question: Which metric most directly surfaces stuck orders?
options: State age percentiles and backlog counts for recoverable states.
Average length of customer shipping addresses.
Frontend bundle parse time for the help center.
Count of employees with admin access.
questions.explanation: You need to know which states are aging past policy.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with explicit compensation flows instead of distributed transactions?
options: They are more practical across services but allow temporary inconsistency windows.
They remove all operator complexity from the system.
They require fewer states than a simple workflow.
They guarantee synchronous rollback of external providers.
questions.explanation: Compensation is realistic, but not instant perfection.

questions.question: What is the right reaction to an ambiguous payment timeout where final processor outcome is unknown?
options: Model an explicit uncertain state and reconcile with the external system before finalizing.
Treat the payment as failed immediately and reuse the same charge reference.
Treat the payment as successful immediately and ship the order.
Delete the timeout event so dashboards stay clean.
questions.explanation: Unknown outcomes should stay explicit until confirmed.

questions.question: Why does operator tooling belong in the design at senior level?
options: Because rare stuck workflows still need safe inspection and recovery when automation stalls.
Because operator tooling makes tests unnecessary.
Because manual actions are always faster than automation.
Because workflow history is useless without a human approving every step.
questions.explanation: Operational recovery is part of the system, not an afterthought.
